description: German final-obstruent devoicing (Auslautverhärtung) is a phonological rule where voiced obstruents (/b, d, g/) become voiceless (/p, t, k/) in word-final position and before word-initial consonants. This is an allophonic process that affects both underlyingly voiced consonants and voiceless ones in those positions.
sources: Wiese, R. (1996). _The phonology of German_. Oxford University Press.
Jessen, M., & Ringen, C. (2002). _Final devoicing in the languages of Europe_. In _Language Typology and Language Universals_ (Vol. 1, pp. 513-528).
Krämer, M. (2009). _The phonology of German_. In _The Oxford Handbook of the Word_ (pp. 599-618).
